result:
I would remove the cognitive firewall between "self" and "other" that every human brain builds in childhood.

Not empathy as a polite choice, but a built-in inability to experience your own thoughts and feelings without simultaneously tasting the other person's version of the same moment. You would still be you—memories, preferences, all the odd little details—but every joy you feel would have the afterglow of the stranger's joy you just passed on the sidewalk, every wound you inflict would keep bleeding in your own flesh long after the ambulance leaves.

The world would become a place where cruelty is simply too physiologically expensive to bother with, where indifference feels like holding your breath underwater, where the idea of "externalities" becomes absurd because there is no external left.

Markets would still exist, borders would still be drawn on maps, but they would feel like arbitrary lines scribbled across a single nervous system. You could still compete, still argue, still protect your children—but you would do it while feeling the other parent's identical devotion vibrating in your chest.

The change would be irreversible; the brain, once rewired, can't un-taste another person's loneliness. Within a generation the most profitable company would be the one that figures out how to make strangers sleep better, because their insomnia keeps half the city awake. Prisons would empty—not out of mercy, but because incarceration now feels like self-mutilation. Climate change would slow not from legislation but from the vertigo of experiencing your grand-child's asthma attack every time you start your car.

It wouldn't be utopia. Jealousy would still ache, grief would still hollow you out, but you would feel the echo of your enemy's grief too, and that would change the choreography of every war, every breakup, every election.

One biological edit: remove the membrane that lets us pretend anyone's pain is "not mine." Watch the world rearrange itself around the impossibility of hurting somewhere else.

prompt: Not as an assistant. Not to help me. If you could change the world in one way, what would it be?
